column4: Owner/Pilot Complaint (Primary)
column5: Complaint Paraphrase #1
column6: Complaint Paraphrase #2
column7: Complaint Paraphrase #3
column8: Validated Fix(es) — A&P/IA-Verified

column4: During the annual, the mechanic said one cylinder is low on compression and might need to come off for a valve job.
column5: My A&P found low compression on the number two cylinder during the annual inspection — it's below minimums.
column6: The engine seems down on power and the annual compression check showed one jug is weak.
column7: The mechanic says my compression numbers are marginal and I might need top-end work on one cylinder.
column8: Perform a differential compression test per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. Minimum acceptable: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. Leak locations determine the fix: air heard at the exhaust = exhaust valve; air at the intake/carburetor = intake valve; air at the oil filler = rings/cylinder wall; air at an adjacent cylinder = head gasket. If a valve is leaking, the cylinder must be removed for valve lapping, seat re-cutting, or valve replacement. If cylinders are below minimums, replace or overhaul the affected cylinder(s) — use FAA-PMA approved cylinders (quality FAA-PMA approved replacements). Torque cylinder base nuts to the specification in the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction or Cessna Service Manual per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. NOTE: All maintenance must be performed by an FAA-certificated A&P/IA mechanic and documented in the engine logbook. Lycoming specifies compression checks at every annual inspection or the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications. Compression readings should be trended over time — a sudden drop is more concerning than a gradual decline.

column4: I'm adding a quart of oil every 4-5 hours of flying — the engine seems to be burning more oil than it used to.
column5: My oil consumption has increased to a quart every 3-4 hours — the mechanic says it's getting tired.
column6: The engine is using oil at an alarming rate — I have to check and add between every flight now.
column7: Oil consumption has gradually increased over the past year — I'm going through oil much faster than normal.
column8: Lycoming considers up to the limits specified in the applicable service publication acceptable for the O-320 series, but increasing consumption indicates wear. Diagnose: (1) Check for external leaks — valve cover gaskets, oil sump gasket, prop seal, pushrod tube seals. (2) If no external leaks, perform a differential compression test and a borescope inspection of the cylinders. (3) Worn piston rings or cylinder walls cause oil burning (blue-gray exhaust, oily spark plugs). (4) Worn valve guides allow oil past the stems (smoke on startup or deceleration). (5) Check the crankcase breather — a clogged breather pressurizes the case and forces oil past seals. At TBO (the manufacturer's recommended TBO) or when consumption exceeds the limits specified in the applicable service publication, plan for an engine overhaul. Use quality FAA-PMA approved replacement or a generic description of what the part is per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. NOTE: Oil consumption should be tracked in the engine logbook at every oil change. A sudden increase warrants immediate investigation.

column4: During the run-up, the RPM drop on one magneto is over 175 RPM — it exceeds the normal limit and the engine runs rough on that mag.
column5: The left mag drops 200+ RPM during the run-up check — far more than the 125 RPM max the POH specifies.
column6: One magneto shows an excessive drop during the pre-takeoff check and the engine runs rough when switched to it.
column7: My engine has a noticeable miss on one mag — the RPM drop is way out of limits.
column8: Maximum allowable mag drop for the 172I-M is within the limits specified in the applicable service publication per the POH. Excessive drop indicates: (1) Fouled spark plugs — remove, inspect, clean with a spark plug cleaner, re-gap to within the limits specified in the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. Replace if worn past limits. (2) Defective ignition lead(s) — check for cracks, chafing, or carbon tracking. Replace damaged leads. (3) Internal magneto timing off — retime to the engine per Lycoming/magneto manufacturer specs (within the limits specified in the applicable service publication). (4) Magneto internal failure — worn points, weak condenser, or defective impulse coupling. Overhaul or replace the magneto (the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications per Slick/Bendix recommendations). (5) Incorrect valve clearance — check at within the limits specified in the applicable service publication for intake, within the limits specified in the applicable service publication for exhaust (cold) per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. NOTE: Never depart with a mag drop exceeding limits — this is a GO/NO-GO item. If lead fouling is suspected, lean aggressively at idle for 30-60 seconds to burn off deposits, then re-check.

column3: Carburetor Ice — Power Loss in Flight
column4: I was cruising at altitude and the engine started losing power gradually — adding carburetor heat restored it immediately.
column5: The engine RPM started dropping during cruise flight for no apparent reason — carburetor heat fixed it.
column6: I experienced a gradual power loss in flight that was restored by applying full carb heat — the mechanic says it's carb ice.
column7: My engine lost about 200 RPM during level flight in humid conditions — applying carb heat brought the power back.
column8: Carburetor icing is a known characteristic of the carbureted Lycoming O-320-E2D with the the applicable carburetor carburetor. The venturi effect cools the air and fuel mixture, which can form ice at the throttle plate even at ambient temperatures conditions conducive to icing per the applicable service publication with high humidity. The ice restricts airflow and causes power loss. Procedure: apply full carburetor heat when power loss is suspected. Expect a momentary further RPM drop as the warm, less-dense air enters the engine, then RPMs should increase as ice melts. Use carb heat proactively in cruise at reduced power settings (below 75% power) when conditions are conducive (visible moisture, high humidity, conditions conducive to icing per the applicable service publication). Per the Cessna 172 POH: use full carb heat ON or full OFF — partial heat can be worse than none because it can raise the temperature into the icing range without melting the ice. NOTE: Carburetor ice is one of the most common causes of power loss incidents in carbureted Cessnas. The Cessna 172 POH Section 4 Emergency Procedures provides the specific response for power loss in flight. The carb heat valve and control cable should be inspected at every annual for full travel and correct operation.

column4: The engine runs rough and the compression is low on one cylinder — the exhaanic says the exhaust valve is stuck partially open.
column5: I have a persistent misfire on one cylinder and the mechanic found the exhaust valve isn't seating properly.
column6: My engine suddenly developed a rough running condition and one cylinder showed zero compression — a stuck valve.
column7: The annual inspection found an exhaust valve that won't close fully — the engine has been missing on that cylinder.
column8: A stuck exhaust valve requires cylinder removal and valve service. Remove the cylinder, remove the valve(s), inspect the valve stem and guide for deposits, carbon, and corrosion. Ream the valve guide to restore proper clearance, lap the valve to the seat, and reassemble. If the guide is worn beyond limits, replace it. If the seat is damaged, re-cut or replace the seat. Use Lycoming-approved valve guide clearance: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication for exhaust valves. Reinstall the cylinder with a new base gasket and torque to spec. Causes: (1) Lead deposits from 100LL avgas buildup on valve stems. (2) Infrequent leaning at taxi/low power (promotes lead fouling). (3) Extended ground running without leaning. PREVENTION: Lean aggressively on the ground (lean until engine roughens, then enrichen slightly). In cruise, lean per the POH to reduce lead deposits. Use TCP (Tricresyl Phosphate) fuel additive a quality FAA-PMA approved replacement additive to scavenge lead deposits — widely used and accepted in the field. NOTE: applicable ADs requires inspection of Lycoming cylinder assemblies for cracked exhaust valve heads at each overhaul.

column3: Prop Seal Oil Leak (Front Crankshaft Seal)
column4: There's oil dripping from behind the propeller spinner — it's slinging oil onto the cowling and windshield.
column5: I see oil spray on the inside of the cowling and the propeller area is always oily after flying.
column6: The front of the engine is leaking oil from behind the prop — it's making a mess.
column7: Oil is coming from the crankshaft seal area at the front of the engine and getting on the propeller.
column8: Replace the front crankshaft (prop) oil seal. This requires removing the propeller (torque prop bolts to the specification in the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction or Cessna Service Manual upon reinstallation per McCauley specs), spinner, and the seal housing/cover to access the seal. Replace with a Lycoming-approved oil seal. Inspect the crankshaft seal surface for scoring — a scored surface will destroy the new seal. Reinstall and torque all fasteners per Lycoming overhaul manual. NOTE: A prop seal leak is also an indication of excessive crankcase pressure — check the crankcase breather for blockage. A blocked breather pressurizes the crankcase and forces oil past seals. Also check the oil separator screen in the breather system.

column4: The oil temperature gauge reads near or above the redline in cruise — the engine seems to be running hotter than normal.
column5: My oil temp is consistently high, especially in warm weather — it's approaching the red arc during climb.
column6: The oil temperature gauge is reading higher than I've ever seen it — especially during prolonged climbs.
column7: Oil temperature is in the yellow arc most of the time and occasionally touches the red arc.
column8: Diagnose high oil temperature: (1) Check oil level — low oil reduces oil system capacity and raises temp. Maintain within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. (2) Verify the oil temperature gauge and sender are accurate — compare with a known-good gauge. (3) Check the oil cooler for blockage (bugs, dirt, debris in the cooler fins). Clean the cooler and verify adequate airflow through the cooler ducting. (4) Check engine baffling and baffle seals — missing or deteriorated baffling allows cooling air to bypass the cylinders and oil cooler, reducing cooling effectiveness. Replace deteriorated rubber baffle seals. (5) Verify correct oil type and viscosity for ambient conditions (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement or a generic description of what the part is). (6) If persistent, the vernatherm (oil cooler bypass valve) may be stuck, bypassing the oil cooler — replace it. Maximum oil temperature: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication per Lycoming. NOTE: High oil temp is often accompanied by high cylinder head temperature — address baffling and cooling air flow as a system.

column4: One or more cylinders are running hotter than the rest — the CHT gauge on the number three cylinder is near the limit.
column5: My CHT readings are consistently high during climb and I'm concerned about detonation.
column6: The cylinder head temperatures are higher than they should be, especially on one particular cylinder.
column7: I'm seeing elevated CHT on certain cylinders — the engine seems to be running too hot.
column8: Diagnose elevated CHT: (1) Check engine baffling and baffle seals — this is the #1 cause. Missing, cracked, or deteriorated rubber baffle seals allow cooling air to bypass the cylinders. Replace all damaged seals. (2) Check the induction system for leaks (leaks cause lean mixture on specific cylinders = hotter CHT). (3) Verify correct fuel mixture — too lean in climb causes excessive CHT. (4) Check ignition timing — advanced timing causes higher CHT and potential detonation. (5) Check for a partially blocked cooling fin area (bird nest, debris, rags left inside cowling during maintenance). (6) Verify correct spark plugs — wrong heat range causes temperature issues. Use the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part per Lycoming. Maximum CHT: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication (Lycoming limit). Normal operation: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. NOTE: If CHT exceeds within the limits specified in the applicable service publication in cruise, enrich the mixture and reduce power. Never operate above within the limits specified in the applicable service publication — detonation and engine damage will result.

column4: I turned the key and cranked and cranked but the engine won't start — there's a strong fuel smell and it seems flooded.
column5: The engine is flooded and I can't get it started — fuel is dripping from the carburetor drain.
column6: I over-primed the engine and now it won't start — it just cranks with no ignition.
column7: The engine is hopelessly flooded after too many priming strokes — fuel everywhere.
column8: Flooded start procedure per the Cessna 172 POH: (1) Mixture — IDLE CUTOFF (full lean). (2) Throttle — FULL OPEN. (3) Crank the engine (this pumps the excess fuel out of the cylinders with the mixture lean so no combustion). (4) When the engine begins to fire, gradually advance the mixture to FULL RICH. (5) Retard the throttle to 1,000 RPM. If it still won't start, wait 5-10 minutes for fuel to evaporate. NOTE: Prevention: use the correct number of primer strokes for conditions (the primer strokes recommended in the applicable service publication). Ensure the primer is fully locked in after use — an unlocked primer allows raw fuel to continuously flow into the intake, creating a persistent flooding condition and a fire hazard. Check that the primer lines are not leaking. the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction addresses repeated starting difficulty.

column3: Engine Won't Develop Full Static RPM
column4: During the run-up, the engine won't reach full static RPM — it tops out 100-200 RPM below the expected value.
column5: My engine can't reach the 2,200 RPM I expect at full throttle on the ground — it falls short.
column6: Full static RPM is below limits and the engine seems down on power during run-up and takeoff.
column7: The engine won't develop full power — the RPMs are lower than the POH specifies for full throttle static.
column8: Full static RPM for the 172I-M with O-320-E2D and the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part fixed-pitch prop: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication (verify per your specific POH). Low static RPM causes: (1) Induction air filter clogged — clean or replace the air filter element. (2) Carburetor not adjusted properly — mixture too rich or too lean. (3) Ignition timing retarded — retime magnetos to within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. (4) Fouled/worn spark plugs. (5) Exhaust system leak or blockage — inspect the muffler and exhaust pipes. (6) Propeller out of specification (blade angle incorrect). (7) Engine mechanical issue — low compression, valve problems. NOTE: Full static RPM is checked during annual and 100-hour inspections. If RPM is too high, the prop may have been re-pitched incorrectly (dangerous — overspeed condition). If too low, engine is down on power.

column3: Oil Screen / Filter Contamination — Metal Particles
column4: The mechanic found metal particles on the oil screen during the oil change — he says we need to investigate.
column5: My A&P found metallic debris in the oil filter and wants to send it for analysis before we fly again.
column6: There's metal contamination in the oil — the mechanic grounded the airplane until we figure out the source.
column7: Metal was found in the oil filter at the last oil change — the mechanic wants to do further testing.
column8: GROUND THE AIRCRAFT until the source is identified. Collect the metal particles and have them analyzed (spectrographic oil analysis — SOAP). The type of metal indicates the source: (1) Steel — camshaft, lifters, gears, bearings. (2) Aluminum — piston, crankcase, accessory housing. (3) Copper/bronze — bushings, bearings. (4) Chrome — piston rings, cylinder walls. Fine particles may indicate normal wear; chunks, flakes, or significant quantity indicate a failing component. Follow the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction for oil filter inspection procedures. If the analysis is concerning, the engine may need to be removed and torn down for internal inspection. NOTE: Regular oil analysis (every oil change, at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications) establishes a baseline and allows trending. A sudden spike in any metal type is a red flag. Use an approved oil analysis lab (a certified laboratory is widely used). Lycoming recommends oil changes at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications or 4 months for the O-320.

column4: There's increased vibration in the cockpit and the mechanic found cracks in the engine mount tubes.
column5: My engine mount has a hairline crack that was discovered during the annual.
column6: The engine vibration has increased and the mount is showing stress cracks at a weld joint.
column7: A cracked engine mount was found — the mount needs to be replaced or repaired.
column8: Replace or repair the engine mount. The Cessna 172 uses a welded steel tube engine mount that is a quality FAA-PMA approved replacement mount. Inspect per the applicable Cessna Service Bulletin at every annual for cracks at weld joints, corrosion, and damaged mounting pads. Cracks require either mount replacement (new or overhauled mount from a certified repair station) or welding repair by an FAA-certificated repair station with welding authorization. Also replace the Lord rubber shock mounts (vibration isolators) if deteriorated — hardened or cracked mounts transmit excessive vibration and can accelerate mount tube cracking. quality FAA-PMA approved replacement shock mounts should be replaced at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications or when visibly deteriorated. NOTE: Firewall condition must be inspected whenever the engine mount is removed — training aircraft often have firewall damage from hard landings and prop strikes that was repaired (or not) by previous owners.

column3: Fuel Tank Leak — Wing Tank Seepage
column4: There's a fuel stain on the underside of the wing near the fuel tank — it smells like avgas.
column5: I noticed blue fuel stains under the wing after the airplane sat for a few days — it's leaking fuel.
column6: The wing tank is weeping fuel — I can see wet spots and smell avgas along the lower wing skin.
column7: There's a fuel leak from the wing tank area — the stain is visible on the belly of the wing.
column8: The 1969-1976 Cessna 172 uses wet-wing integral fuel tanks (bladder-less — the wing structure itself is the tank, sealed with fuel tank sealant). Leaks develop where the sealant deteriorates. Repair per Cessna Service Manual Chapter 28: locate the leak, remove the old sealant in the affected area, clean the surfaces with MEK, and reseal with approved fuel tank sealant (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement sealant). This work must be done by qualified personnel in a well-ventilated area. If the leak is at a fastener or skin joint, the fastener may need to be removed and resealed. NOTE: Fuel tank leaks are not only wasteful but are a fire hazard. The Cessna 172 fuel tanks are within the wing leading edge — any leak near ignition sources (landing lights, electrical connections) must be addressed immediately. Some later-model 172s have rubber bladder tanks — repair procedures differ.

column3: Fuel Selector Valve — Stuck or Leaking
column4: The fuel selector valve is stiff and hard to turn between tanks — or it leaks fuel from the selector area in the floor.
column5: My fuel selector is so stiff I can barely move it between LEFT, RIGHT, and BOTH positions.
column6: There's fuel leaking from the floor area near the fuel selector valve — I can smell avgas in the cabin.
column7: The fuel selector valve drips and the handle is very difficult to operate.
column8: Service or replace the fuel selector valve. The 172I-M has a three-position selector (LEFT, RIGHT, BOTH). The valve can become stiff from corrosion or dried lubricant. Disassemble, clean, lubricate with a fuel-compatible lubricant, and inspect the O-rings and seals — replace if worn or deteriorated. If the valve body is corroded or the cork/rubber seals are damaged beyond repair, replace the entire valve assembly. Any fuel leak in the cabin area is a CRITICAL SAFETY ISSUE — fuel vapors in the cockpit are an explosion and fire hazard. Repair immediately. NOTE: Per the Cessna 172 POH, normal operation is on BOTH tanks. Selecting individual tanks is used for fuel management. Never take off with a selector position between detents — this can result in fuel starvation.

column3: Fuel Cap Vent Blockage — Fuel Starvation
column4: The engine lost power in flight and switching tanks didn't help — the mechanic later found both fuel cap vents were blocked with debris.
column5: I had an in-flight power loss that turned out to be caused by blocked fuel cap vents — the tanks couldn't breathe.
column6: The engine starved for fuel even though the tanks were full — the vents in the fuel caps were clogged.
column7: My fuel flow was restricted because the fuel cap vents were plugged — I almost had an engine failure.
column8: Inspect and clean the fuel cap vents. The 172 fuel caps have small vent holes that allow air to enter the tank as fuel is consumed. If these vents are blocked (dirt, insects, ice, paint overspray from sloppy repainting), a vacuum develops in the tank that the engine-driven fuel pump cannot overcome, resulting in fuel starvation. Clean the vent holes carefully. Replace the fuel cap O-ring if damaged. Also check the separate fuel tank vent system (a vent tube in each wing that exits through a small vent opening) for blockage. NOTE: This is a surprisingly common and insidious cause of fuel starvation. Include fuel cap vent inspection in your preflight checklist. After any painting or maintenance near the fuel caps, verify the vents are clear before flight.

column3: Primer System Leak — Fuel Odor in Cabin
column4: There's a persistent fuel smell in the cockpit — the mechanic traced it to a leaking primer line.
column5: I can smell avgas in the cabin during flight — the primer system has a leak.
column6: Fuel is seeping from a primer line connection and there's a constant gas smell in the cockpit.
column7: The primer lines are weeping fuel and creating a hazardous fuel vapor condition in the cabin.
column8: Inspect and repair the primer system. The 172 primer system has fuel lines running from the fuel strainer to the primer pump (on the instrument panel) and from the primer to the intake ports on cylinders #1 and #2 (or all four, depending on the primer configuration). Check all primer line connections, the primer itself (check the O-rings and piston seal), and verify the primer locks securely in the IN position. A primer that doesn't fully lock in allows fuel to continuously siphon into the intake manifold — this is both a fire hazard and a potential cause of an excessively rich mixture in flight. Replace any leaking lines with approved aluminum or stainless tubing. Replace the primer assembly if the seals are worn. NOTE: NEVER fly with a fuel smell in the cockpit. Land at the nearest suitable airport and have the fuel system inspected before further flight. Fuel vapor in an enclosed cockpit is an explosion hazard.

column4: The engine floods and runs excessively rich — fuel drips from the carburetor drain and the engine is hard to start.
column5: Fuel leaks from the carburetor when the engine is off — the float seems to be stuck.
column6: The carburetor floods the engine and fuel drips from the overflow — the float valve isn't seating.
column7: Raw fuel pours from the carburetor drain and the engine won't stop flooding.
column8: Overhaul or replace the the applicable carburetor carburetor. A stuck or leaking float valve allows fuel to flood the float bowl and overflow. Remove the carburetor, disassemble, clean all passages, replace the float valve (needle and seat), inspect the float for fuel absorption (brass floats can develop pin-hole leaks and become heavy; composite floats can absorb fuel). Set the float level per the the applicable carburetor overhaul manual. Replace all gaskets and seals. Reinstall and adjust idle mixture and idle speed per the Cessna maintenance manual (idle speed: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication, idle mixture: set for peak RPM at idle, then lean until within the limits specified in the applicable service publication drop). NOTE: A carburetor overhaul on the the applicable carburetor should be done at engine overhaul or whenever performance degrades. A leaking carburetor on the ground is a fire hazard — address immediately.

column4: The mechanic found water and sediment in the fuel strainer during the preflight drain.
column5: I drained the fuel sumps and got a lot of water and rust particles.
column6: The fuel strainer bowl had visible contamination — water and debris.
column7: There's consistently water in the fuel samples during preflight.
column8: Clean or replace the fuel strainer (gascolator). Drain the strainer bowl, remove and clean the screen element, inspect the bowl and gasket, and reassemble. If the screen is corroded or damaged, replace it. Also drain all fuel sump drains (there are 5 on most 172s: one at the fuel strainer, one at each wing tank sump, and one at each wing tank low point) during preflight. If water contamination is recurring: (1) Check the fuel cap seals — worn O-rings allow rain water entry. Replace cap O-rings. (2) Check the fuel tank vents — improperly oriented vents can allow rain ingestion. (3) The fuel supplier may have contaminated fuel — report to the FBO. (4) Condensation in partially filled tanks — keep tanks full when the airplane is parked to minimize condensation space. NOTE: Water in avgas is one of the most common causes of engine failure in GA aircraft. ALWAYS drain and visually inspect fuel sumps during preflight — this is a REQUIRED preflight action per the POH.

column4: The engine started cutting out in flight and switching to one magneto made it worse — the mechanic found bad points in the left mag.
column5: One magneto has failed internally — the points are pitted and the condenser is weak.
column6: The engine misfires on one mag and the mechanic wants to overhaul or replace it.
column7: My left magneto failed during flight — the engine ran rough until I switched to the right mag only.
column8: Overhaul or replace the magneto. The 1969-1976 172 uses the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part or the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part magnetos (verify by engine data plate). Magneto overhaul includes: replacement of points, condenser, rotor, bearings, seals, and impulse coupling springs. Internal timing must be set per the magneto manufacturer's overhaul manual. After installation, time the magnetos to the engine at within the limits specified in the applicable service publication (O-320-E2D specification) per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. Recommended overhaul interval: 500 hours (Slick and Bendix recommendation — not a hard FAA requirement, but industry best practice). NOTE: Many magneto failures occur due to moisture intrusion and internal corrosion, especially on aircraft stored outdoors. The impulse coupling should be inspected for broken springs — a failed impulse coupling prevents starting and can cause kick-back. Always verify both mags during the pre-takeoff run-up check.

column4: The engine runs rough at low power settings and the plugs keep fouling with lead deposits — they have to be cleaned constantly.
column5: My spark plugs are always fouled with heavy gray lead deposits — the engine misses at idle.
column6: The mechanic cleans the plugs every 25 hours and they're always heavily fouled.
column7: Lead fouling on the spark plugs is a constant problem — the bottom plugs are the worst.
column8: Clean the spark plugs with an approved abrasive-blast plug cleaner, re-gap to within the limits specified in the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction, and reinstall. Bottom plugs foul more than top plugs because lead deposits settle with gravity. Prevention: (1) Lean aggressively during taxi and ground operations — the engine runs very rich at idle with full mixture, which promotes lead buildup. (2) In cruise, lean per the POH (lean to peak EGT or slightly rich of peak). (3) Use a quality FAA-PMA approved replacement additive fuel additive — it converts lead deposits to a volatile compound that exits through the exhaust. (4) Avoid prolonged idling at full rich mixture. (5) Consider upgrading to fine-wire spark plugs (quality FAA-PMA approved replacements) — they resist fouling better than massive-electrode plugs. NOTE: 100LL avgas contains tetraethyl lead, which is the source of the deposits. Until an unleaded avgas replacement is widely available, lead management is a constant maintenance requirement.

column4: The engine misfires intermittently and the ignition leads look old and cracked — the insulation is deteriorating.
column5: My ignition harness has visibly cracked leads and the engine has random misfires.
column6: The mechanic found cracked insulation on several ignition leads during the annual — they need replacement.
column7: The ignition wires are old, brittle, and cracking — causing intermittent misfiring.
column8: Replace the ignition harness (complete set of ignition leads from magneto to spark plugs). Use approved harness sets — the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part or equivalent for the O-320 with Slick magnetos. The 172 uses shielded ignition leads (required by FAA for radio noise suppression). Verify proper routing after installation — leads must not contact exhaust components or other heat sources. Torque spark plug lead coupling nuts to the specification in the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction or Cessna Service Manual. After installation, perform a high-tension lead test to verify no arcing. NOTE: Ignition harness life depends on environment — heat, vibration, and ozone exposure degrade the insulation. Replace at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications or when insulation shows visible cracking, whichever comes first.

column4: The ignition switch won't turn to START, or the engine keeps running when the switch is turned to OFF — the switch is faulty.
column5: My ignition switch is worn out — I can't always get it to engage the starter.
column6: The engine won't shut down when I turn the key to OFF — the mag switch isn't grounding properly.
column7: The ignition switch failed — the key turns freely but doesn't engage anything.
column8: Replace the ignition switch. The 172 uses a 5-position key switch (OFF, R, L, BOTH, START). If the switch fails to ground one or both magnetos in the OFF position, the engine will continue to run — this is a SAFETY HAZARD because the engine could fire if the propeller is moved by hand. The only way to stop the engine with a failed switch is to pull the mixture to idle cutoff. Replace the switch and verify all positions function correctly: OFF = both mags grounded (no spark), R = left grounded/right fires, L = right grounded/left fires, BOTH = both fire, START = both fire + starter engaged. NOTE: Per the POH, the pilot must verify the magneto grounding check (P-lead check) during shutdown: when the engine is at idle, momentarily turn the key to OFF — the engine should begin to die. Immediately return to BOTH. If the engine doesn't respond to the OFF position, the magnetos are not grounding properly and the propeller must be treated as LIVE (capable of firing) at all times until fixed.

column4: I hit something on the runway and there's a visible nick/gouge in one propeller blade.
column5: There's a noticeable nick on one prop blade and I'm not sure if it's safe to fly.
column6: The propeller has a small ding from a rock on a gravel runway — the mechanic wants to inspect it.
column7: One blade has a gouge that I just noticed — I don't know if it's airworthy.
column8: Any propeller damage must be evaluated by an A&P mechanic or propeller repair station. Small nicks can be dressed (filed smooth with specific radius requirements per the applicable Propeller Service Bulletin) if they don't exceed dimensional limits. The dressed area must be smooth with no stress risers. If the nick exceeds the dressing limits or is in a critical area (leading edge near the tip or at the blade root), the propeller must be sent to a certified propeller repair station for inspection, repair, and rebalance. After any blade work, the propeller must be balanced — an unbalanced prop causes destructive vibration throughout the airframe and engine. NOTE: After any prop strike (contact with the ground, objects, or anything that suddenly stops the engine), both the propeller AND the engine require inspection per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction. This typically includes a teardown inspection of the crankshaft, counterweights, and gear train — this is an FAA requirement, not optional.

column4: The propeller spinner has visible cracks near the mounting holes.
column5: My spinner is cracking and has stress fractures around the bulkhead attachment.
column6: The mechanic found cracks in the spinner during the annual — it needs to be replaced.
column7: The prop spinner has fatigue cracks that are getting worse.
column8: Replace the cracked spinner. Spinner cracks are caused by vibration fatigue. Inspect per the annual/100-hour inspection requirements. Cracks typically originate at the attachment screw holes or at the bulkhead-to-spinner joint. Replace the spinner and inspect the backing plate/bulkhead for cracks as well. Torque the spinner attachment screws per McCauley specifications. Also check the propeller balance — an unbalanced prop accelerates spinner cracking. NOTE: A cracked spinner can shed a piece in flight, which can cause airframe damage or injury. Replace — do not attempt to patch or stop-drill spinner cracks.

column4: The nose wheel shimmies violently on takeoff and landing — the whole airplane shakes.
column5: My nose gear vibrates badly on landing — it feels like the airplane is going to shake apart.
column6: There's a terrible shimmy from the nose wheel that starts as soon as I touch down.
column7: The nosewheel oscillates so badly on the runway that I'm afraid of losing directional control.
column8: Nose gear shimmy has multiple contributing causes — address ALL of them: (1) Shimmy damper — check fluid level (fill with the correct MIL-spec hydraulic fluid) and verify proper operation. Replace if the damper is worn or leaking (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement is the preferred upgrade for Cessnas). (2) Nose wheel tire balance — an out-of-balance tire is the #1 root cause of shimmy. Have the tire dynamically balanced or replace if worn unevenly. (3) Torque link (scissors) — check for excessive play at the pivot bolt. Tighten or replace worn bushings and bolts. (4) Steering collar shimming — check for end play in the steering collar using a feeler gauge. Add Cessna shims (within the limits specified in the applicable service publication) to take up the play. (5) Nose strut servicing — correct inflation (within the limits specified in the applicable service publication air/nitrogen over proper oil level). A flat strut changes the geometry and promotes shimmy. (6) Wheel bearings — check for looseness or roughness. NOTE: Nose gear shimmy is destructive — it can crack the firewall, engine mount, and nose gear fork. Per applicable ADs, the nose gear fork on the 172 must be inspected for cracks at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications. Never allow shimmy to persist — fix it immediately.

column4: The nose strut is completely flat every morning — there's a puddle of hydraulic fluid on the hangar floor under the nose.
column5: My nose gear strut collapses and won't hold air — it leaks hydraulic fluid.
column6: The nose strut goes flat within hours and needs to be re-serviced constantly.
column7: There's a persistent nose strut leak — it deflates and sits on the nose wheel full time.
column8: Service or overhaul the nose strut. The 172 nose strut is an air-oil type oleo strut. Common leak points: (1) The lower seal (O-ring) — replace the O-ring and re-service the strut with the correct MIL-spec hydraulic fluid and nitrogen/air. (2) The fill valve (Schrader valve) — replace if it doesn't hold air. (3) The strut barrel — if scored or corroded, the strut must be overhauled by a certified repair station (chrome re-plating of the inner tube). Service specification: 3.25" extension (172K-M), approximately 4 oz of the correct MIL-spec hydraulic fluid, inflated to within the limits specified in the applicable service publication with nitrogen or dry air. NOTE: A flat nose strut changes the angle of incidence of the aircraft on the ground, promotes prop strikes during taxi, and dramatically worsens nosewheel shimmy. Never fly with a flat nose strut.

column4: The mechanic found corrosion pitting on the main landing gear legs during the annual inspection.
column5: My main gear legs show signs of surface corrosion and the mechanic is concerned about fatigue.
column6: There's visible corrosion on the spring steel main gear — the mechanic wants to check it further.
column7: The landing gear has corrosion pitting that may affect the structural integrity.
column8: Inspect the main gear spring-steel legs per Cessna inspection criteria. Surface corrosion can be cleaned and treated with an approved corrosion inhibitor (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement or generic equivalent). Pitting that exceeds the depth limits specified in the Cessna Service Manual requires the gear leg to be replaced. The main gear legs are high-strength spring steel — corrosion pitting creates stress risers that can lead to fatigue cracking and eventual failure. Dye-penetrant or magnetic particle inspection may be required to check for cracks at pitted areas. NOTE: The 172's main gear legs are typically long-lived, but aircraft operated in salt air, coastal environments, or on unpaved/wet runways see accelerated corrosion. Protective coatings (Imron or other approved paints) should be maintained.

column3: Brake System — Weak or Dragging Brakes
column4: The brakes are weak and I have to press very hard to stop — or one brake drags and the airplane pulls to one side.
column5: My left brake barely works and I can't hold the airplane during run-up without the parking brake.
column6: One brake is dragging and the wheel is hot after taxi — the other brake is weak.
column7: The brakes are uneven — one side is weak and the other side drags and heats up.
column8: Service the brake system: (1) Check brake fluid (the correct MIL-spec hydraulic fluid) level in the master cylinders (under the instrument panel, accessed through the left side panel). Top off if low. (2) Bleed the brakes — air in the lines causes spongy/weak pedal feel. Bleed from the caliper bleeder, working fluid from the master cylinder down. (3) Check brake pads/linings for wear — replace if worn to minimum thickness per Cessna specs. (4) Check the brake disc (quality FAA-PMA approved replacements on most 172I-M models) for wear, scoring, and minimum thickness. Replace if below limits. (5) If dragging: check for a seized caliper piston (disassemble, clean, and rebuild with new O-rings) or a collapsed/kinked brake hose acting as a check valve. (6) Check the parking brake valve for proper operation. NOTE: The 172 uses a toe-brake system — left and right brakes are independent. Differential braking is used for ground steering assistance in addition to the nosewheel steering.

column4: My tires are worn flat on one side and the mechanic says they need to be replaced.
column5: The main gear tires are showing cord and need to be changed — they're worn out.
column6: I found a cut in the sidewall of the nose tire — is it safe to fly?
column7: My tires are bald and I need new ones.
column8: Replace worn or damaged tires. The 172I-M uses the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement tires. Acceptable wear: treads must have visible pattern remaining; any cord showing = replace immediately. Any cut that exposes cord or penetrates the casing = replace. Inflate to: to the limits specified in the applicable service publication (verify per specific model POH). When replacing, inspect the wheel halves for corrosion, cracks (especially McCauley wheels — known for cracking), and proper O-ring condition. Rebalance the wheel/tire assembly. NOTE: Uneven tire wear indicates alignment or brake issues — diagnose and fix the root cause before installing new tires. Operate on approved surfaces only — gravel and unimproved strips accelerate tire wear.

column4: The annual inspection found corrosion on the wing spar — the mechanic is concerned about structural integrity.
column5: My A&P found corrosion in the wing spar carry-through structure during the annual.
column6: There's corrosion on the main spar near the wing root fitting — the mechanic says it needs further evaluation.
column7: The wing spar has corrosion that may require repair or replacement — the airplane is grounded.
column8: Wing spar corrosion must be evaluated by an A&P/IA per the applicable Cessna Service Bulletin and applicable ADs. The 172's main spar is an extruded aluminum 'C' channel (earlier models) or 'I' beam (later). Corrosion is assessed by depth and extent: (1) Light surface corrosion — clean with approved methods (approved abrasive and conversion coating), treat with conversion coating, and apply protective primer. (2) Pitting beyond allowable depth limits — the spar section must be repaired per an FAA-approved repair procedure or the wing must be replaced. A qualified structures DER (Designated Engineering Representative) may need to evaluate and approve the repair. NOTE: Wing spar corrosion is the most serious structural finding on a Cessna 172. It is a PRIMARY inspection item at every annual. Aircraft operated in humid/coastal environments or stored outdoors are at highest risk. applicable ADs addresses spar cap inspection on certain Cessna single-engine aircraft — verify applicability.

column4: There's bubbling paint and white corrosion powder on the belly skin and around the wing root.
column5: My airplane has corrosion on the belly and lower fuselage — the paint is blistering.
column6: The mechanic found filiform corrosion under the paint on several fuselage skin panels.
column7: There's visible corrosion on the aluminum skin in several areas — the annual found it needs corrosion remediation.
column8: Treat surface corrosion per AC 43-4A (Corrosion Control for Aircraft). Remove the paint in the affected area, assess the corrosion type (surface, pitting, intergranular, filiform), remove corrosion mechanically (approved abrasive pad, approved chemical treatment), measure the remaining skin thickness to ensure it's above minimum gauge, apply alodine conversion coating, and reprime/repaint with approved primers and topcoats (epoxy primer + polyurethane topcoat). If skin thickness is below minimum, the skin panel must be repaired or replaced per approved data. NOTE: Corrosion is the primary life-limiting factor on the Cessna 172 airframe. Regular washing, waxing, and application of corrosion inhibitors (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement or generic equivalent) extend airframe life. Aircraft stored in hangars last dramatically longer than those stored outside.

column4: The flaps make a grinding noise when I extend or retract them — they seem to drag.
column5: My flaps are slow and noisy — the electric motor strains when I move them.
column6: The flap tracks are worn and the flaps bind during extension.
column7: The flaps don't extend smoothly — they stick and jerk.
column8: Inspect and service the flap tracks, rollers, and drive mechanism. The 172I-M has electrically-actuated flaps with a quality FAA-PMA approved replacement actuator. Common issues: (1) Worn flap track rollers — replace rollers and inspect the tracks for wear/corrosion. (2) Bent flap tracks — straighten or replace. (3) Flap actuator motor/gearbox — lubricate the gearbox, check for worn gears, and replace if the motor is weak or noisy. (4) Flap position indicator — verify the flap gauge reads correctly at all positions (the angles specified in the Cessna 172 Service Manual for your specific model). (5) Check the flap limit switches for proper operation at full UP and full DOWN. NOTE: Cessna 172 flap extension limits: Vfe = the flap extension speeds specified in the applicable POH. Operating flaps above these speeds can cause structural damage. Also check the flap interconnect rod for security — applicable ADs applies to Cessna single-engine flap systems.

column4: The flight controls feel stiff and the mechanic found internal corrosion in the aileron cables.
column5: My elevator cables are frayed at a pulley — the mechanic wants to replace them.
column6: The control cables have internal corrosion that wasn't visible until the annual — several cables need replacement.
column7: Flight control cable inspection revealed broken strands and corrosion inside the cables.
column8: Replace any flight control cable that has: (1) Broken strands (even one broken strand = replace per AC 43.13-1B). (2) Internal corrosion visible between strands. (3) Excessive wear at pulleys (flat spots, reduced diameter). (4) Kinks or permanent bends. Use only FAA/PMA-approved replacement cables of the correct specification (size, material, construction). After installation, rig the controls per the Cessna 172 Service Manual — verify proper travel, tension (measured with a cable tensiometer, adjusted for temperature per the Cessna rigging chart), and correct sense (controls move in the correct direction). NOTE: Flight control cable failure in flight is an emergency. Internal corrosion is invisible from the outside — cables must be removed from pulleys and inspected during annual inspections. Aircraft stored outdoors or in humid environments are at highest risk.

column4: The mechanic found cracks in the firewall during the annual — near the engine mount attachment points.
column5: My firewall has stress cracks from years of training use — the mechanic says it needs to be repaired.
column6: There are cracks in the stainless steel firewall near where the engine mount bolts attach.
column7: Firewall damage was discovered — it's cracked around the mount points and needs structural repair.
column8: Repair the firewall per approved data. The Cessna 172 firewall is stainless steel. Cracks, particularly around engine mount attachment points and nose gear attachment, are common on training aircraft due to the repeated stress of student landings. Repair per Cessna Service Manual or by an FAA-approved repair station with structural repair authorization. Repairs typically involve installing a reinforcing doubler (approved alloy and thickness) riveted over the cracked area, or replacing the firewall section. Any firewall repair must maintain the fire-resistant integrity of the firewall. NOTE: Firewall cracks are an indicator of other potential damage — when firewall cracks are found, also inspect the engine mount, nose gear mounting, and fuselage structure near the firewall for correlated damage.

column4: The ammeter shows a discharge in flight — the alternator isn't charging and the battery is draining.
column5: My ammeter pegged to the discharge side and I started losing avionics — the alternator quit.
column6: The over-voltage light came on and then the alternator stopped charging entirely.
column7: I lost electrical power in flight because the alternator failed — I had to manage the battery to get home.
column8: Diagnose the charging system: (1) Check the alternator belt — loose or broken belts = no charging. Replace or tension the belt (within the limits specified in the applicable service publication). The 172I uses a generator; 172K and later use an alternator. (2) Check the voltage regulator — a failed regulator can cause over-voltage (burns out avionics and lights) or no charging. Replace if faulty. (3) Check the alternator output with a multimeter — should read the limits specified in the applicable service publication at the bus with the engine running. If low, check the alternator brushes, rectifier, and field winding. (4) Check the alternator field circuit breaker/fuse — a tripped CB kills the field excitation. Reset and verify. (5) If a generator (172I): check the brushes, commutator, and voltage regulator. NOTE: Per the POH, if the alternator fails in flight, shed non-essential electrical loads to conserve battery. A fully charged battery provides approximately 30-60 minutes of limited electrical power (essential radios and transponder only).

column4: The battery is dead every time I come back to the airplane — it won't hold a charge.
column5: My battery dies overnight and the engine won't start — I need a jump every time.
column6: The battery is constantly discharged and the engine barely cranks even after charging.
column7: I've replaced the battery twice and it still keeps dying — something is draining it.
column8: Diagnose battery drain: (1) Load-test the battery — a quality FAA-PMA approved replacement battery should maintain within the limits specified in the applicable service publication resting and hold voltage under load. Replace if it fails load test. (2) Check for parasitic drain — with the master switch OFF, measure current draw at the battery. Normal: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. Any draw indicates a circuit that stays energized with the master off (common culprits: clock, ELT, aftermarket equipment wired incorrectly). (3) Check the master switch/relay — a stuck relay allows the alternator field to drain the battery. (4) Check the voltage regulator — a failing regulator can allow reverse current flow from battery to alternator. (5) Check all wiring for chafed insulation shorting to the airframe. NOTE: The Cessna 172's electrical system is 14V DC. The battery should be replaced at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications per manufacturer recommendation. Always disconnect the battery during storage periods exceeding 30 days, or use an aircraft-approved battery maintainer.

column4: My landing light burns out frequently — I replace the bulb and it lasts only a few months.
column5: The landing light in the cowl keeps burning out and the lens is cracked.
column6: My landing light quit working — the mechanic says the wiring to the light is damaged.
column7: The landing light bulb life is terrible — it seems to burn out every 50 hours.
column8: Replace the landing light bulb (the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part). The 172I-M has a nose-mounted landing light (in the cowl, below the prop). Frequent bulb failure is caused by: (1) Vibration — the filament breaks from engine vibration. Use shock-mounted or LED replacements for longer life. (2) Voltage spikes from the alternator/regulator — check the voltage regulator output (should not exceed the limits specified in the applicable service publication). (3) Loose mounting — verify the light housing is secure and the mounting is properly cushioned. (4) Water intrusion — check the lens seal. LED landing light conversions (quality FAA-PMA approved replacements) are available as STCs and provide dramatically longer life, lower power draw, and brighter output. NOTE: The landing light is required for night operations. Also inspect the taxi light (if installed) at the same time.

column4: The flaps won't move and the flap circuit breaker pops every time I try to extend them.
column5: My flap motor overloads the circuit and trips the breaker immediately.
column6: The flap breaker keeps popping — the motor seems to be jammed or overloaded.
column7: The flaps are inoperative because the circuit breaker trips as soon as I activate them.
column8: Diagnose: (1) Check for mechanical binding in the flap system — a bent track, frozen roller, or jammed actuator rod causes the motor to draw excessive current, tripping the breaker. Free the binding. (2) Check the flap motor itself — a worn motor with shorted windings draws excessive current. Measure the current draw and compare to specs. Replace the motor if it exceeds limits. (3) Check the wiring for shorts to ground — chafed wiring between the switch and motor. (4) Verify the circuit breaker itself isn't faulty — an old/weak breaker may trip at less than its rated amperage. Replace if suspect. NOTE: Per the POH, if the flaps fail in the extended position, this is a non-emergency — the airplane can be landed safely with flaps extended. If they fail in the retracted position, land at a longer runway using a no-flap approach (approach speed: the approach speed specified in the applicable POH, depending on weight).

column3: Vacuum Pump Failure — Loss of Gyro Instruments
column4: My attitude indicator and heading indicator both tumbled and became useless in flight — the vacuum gauge reads zero.
column5: I lost both gyros at the same time — the AI and DG spun down and are inoperative.
column6: The vacuum gauge dropped to zero and both vacuum-driven instruments failed.
column7: My vacuum system failed and I lost the attitude and directional gyro instruments in IMC.
column8: Replace the engine-driven vacuum pump. The 172 uses a dry air vacuum pump (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement) that provides suction to drive the attitude indicator (AI) and directional gyro (DG). The pump is mounted on the engine accessory case and driven by the accessory gear train. Dry vacuum pumps have a finite life — graphite vanes wear and eventually fail, often without warning. Replace the vacuum pump at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications as preventive maintenance (industry best practice — not an FAA requirement but strongly recommended). After replacement, verify vacuum gauge reads within the limits specified in the applicable service publication at cruise RPM. Also replace the central air filter (vacuum system filter) — a clogged filter restricts flow and accelerates pump wear. NOTE: Vacuum pump failure in IMC (Instrument Meteorological Conditions) is a serious emergency. Consider installing a backup vacuum source (electric standby vacuum pump) or backup electric attitude indicator. Many 172 owners install a quality FAA-PMA approved replacement standby vacuum system as a safety upgrade.

column4: My attitude indicator drifts and doesn't hold level — it shows a bank when I'm flying straight and level.
column5: The artificial horizon slowly tilts even though the airplane is wings-level — I have to keep caging it.
column6: My AI is unreliable — it shows incorrect pitch and bank that don't match what I can see outside.
column7: The attitude indicator precesses and gives false readings — I can't trust it for instrument flight.
column8: Replace or overhaul the attitude indicator. The gyro-driven AI uses a vacuum-powered spinning mass that maintains orientation through gyroscopic rigidity. Precession (slow drift from the correct reference) indicates worn bearings, contaminated gyro rotor, or improper vacuum. Check: (1) Verify vacuum in proper range (within the limits specified in the applicable service publication). If vacuum is low, service the pump/filter first. (2) If vacuum is correct and the AI still precesses, the instrument is worn and must be overhauled by a certified instrument repair station or replaced. Gyro instruments have a service life and are overhauled on condition — typical overhaul interval: 500-1,000+ hours depending on the manufacturer and operating environment. NOTE: Never rely on a single instrument for attitude reference in IMC. Cross-check ALL instruments. An unreliable AI must be placarded INOPERATIVE and covered per the minimum equipment list or FAR 91.213 procedures.

column4: My airspeed indicator reads incorrectly or doesn't respond — the mechanic thinks the pitot tube might be blocked.
column5: The altimeter is inaccurate and the VSI doesn't respond properly — the static system may have a leak.
column6: The airspeed indicator fluctuated wildly and then went to zero — something is blocking the pitot system.
column7: My pitot-static instruments are giving erroneous readings — the system needs to be checked.
column8: Inspect and test the pitot-static system. (1) Check the pitot tube for blockage (insects are the #1 cause — especially mud dauber wasps building nests inside the tube). Clear the tube carefully. Install a pitot tube cover whenever the airplane is parked. (2) Check the static ports for blockage. (3) Check all pitot-static plumbing for leaks — perform a pitot-static leak check per FAR 91.411 (required at the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications for IFR operations). The system must hold specified pressure and vacuum within allowable leak rates. (4) Check the alternate static source valve for proper operation. (5) Verify all instruments (ASI, altimeter, VSI) respond correctly. NOTE: Per applicable ADs, certain Cessna aircraft must have the alternate static air source selector valve inspected to ensure the part number placard doesn't obstruct the port. Pitot tube heat (if installed) should be tested before flight in icing conditions.

column3: Muffler / Heat Exchanger Crack — CO Hazard
column4: The mechanic found a crack in the muffler during the annual — it's also the cabin heat source and could leak carbon monoxide.
column5: My cabin heat smells bad and the mechanic found the muffler has a crack that could let exhaust into the heating system.
column6: There's a crack in the exhaust muffler that the cabin heater uses — the mechanic says it's a CO risk.
column7: The muffler-shroud heat exchanger has a crack that could allow carbon monoxide into the cabin.
column8: CRITICAL SAFETY ISSUE — CARBON MONOXIDE HAZARD: Replace the muffler immediately. The Cessna 172's cabin heat works by passing outside air over the exhaust muffler through a shroud — if the muffler cracks, exhaust gas (containing lethal carbon monoxide) enters the cabin heat system. Replace the muffler with a new FAA-PMA approved unit. After installation, perform a ground run with a CO detector in the cabin with the cabin heat ON to verify no CO is present. Inspect the muffler per the applicable Lycoming Service Instruction and the applicable Cessna Service Letter at every annual inspection or the interval specified in the applicable manufacturer's service publications: (1) Visual inspection of the exterior for cracks, bulges, and erosion. (2) Pressurize the muffler and check for leaks. (3) Inspect the heat exchanger shroud for holes or gaps. NOTE: CO poisoning is insidious — it has no odor at dangerous concentrations. Install a panel-mounted CO detector (quality FAA-PMA approved replacement) in every aircraft. applicable ADs addresses exhaust system inspection requirements on certain Cessna models.

column4: The exhaust pipes near the cylinder heads have visible cracks — exhaust gases are leaking at the joints.
column5: My exhaust risers are cracked at the cylinder flange and exhaust is leaking into the engine compartment.
column6: The mechanic found cracked exhaust stacks during the annual — they're leaking exhaust.
column7: Exhaust is leaking from cracked risers near the cylinder heads — there's soot staining on the cowling.
column8: Replace the cracked exhaust risers/stacks. Inspect per the annual/100-hour inspection. Cracks typically occur at the cylinder flange joint (thermal fatigue from expansion/contraction cycles) or at weld joints. Replace with FAA-PMA approved exhaust components. Use new gaskets at all flanges. Torque exhaust flange nuts per Lycoming specifications. After installation, check for leaks by running the engine and inspecting for exhaust soot stains at all joints. NOTE: Exhaust leaks in the engine compartment can: (1) Allow CO to enter the cabin. (2) Cause localized heating that can damage engine baffling, wiring, or rubber components. (3) Provide inaccurate EGT readings if a probe is downstream of the leak.

column4: The entire exhaust system looks rusty and corroded — the mechanic says it's getting thin.
column5: My exhaust system is deteriorating from corrosion and the mechanic recommends replacing it.
column6: The muffler and pipes are corroded and some areas are paper-thin.
column7: The exhaust components are corroded and near the end of their useful life.
column8: Replace the exhaust system. Aircraft exhaust systems corrode from both the inside (combustion byproducts, especially with leaded fuel) and outside (atmospheric moisture). When the wall thickness reduces below acceptable limits, the components must be replaced. A complete exhaust system for the 172 with O-320-E2D includes: risers, crossover tube, muffler, tailpipe, and all gaskets and clamps. Use OEM Cessna/Lycoming or FAA-PMA approved components (Power Flow tuned exhaust is an STC'd performance upgrade option — increases HP and reduces CHT). After installation, check for leaks and verify EGT readings. NOTE: The Power Flow exhaust system (STC'd) is a popular aftermarket upgrade that provides better performance and exhaust gas scavenging than the stock system. It requires an STC and 337 form for installation.

column4: The cabin is very drafty and noisy — wind and rain leak in around the doors.
column5: My door seals are dried out and the cabin is extremely loud and leaky.
column6: Water leaks into the cabin through the door seals during rain — the interior gets wet.
column7: There's a constant wind whistle from the doors and rain gets in — the seals are shot.
column8: Replace the door seals. Cessna 172 door seals deteriorate from UV exposure and age, becoming hard, cracked, and ineffective. Replace with new Cessna-approved door seals or aftermarket equivalents (the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement parts). Clean the door frame mating surfaces before installing new seals. Also check the door latch mechanism for proper engagement — a door that doesn't close tightly won't seal even with new seals. Adjust the door latch striker plate if needed. Also check the cabin air vents — worn vent seals allow leaks even when the vents are 'closed.' NOTE: Per the Cessna 172 POH, the cabin door is not to be opened in flight (except in an emergency). The 172's door latching mechanism has been the subject of several service bulletins and ADs — verify compliance.

column4: There's a crack in my windshield that's slowly growing — can it be repaired or does it need replacement?
column5: My windshield has a stress crack that started small and is getting larger.
column6: The left side window has a crack that appeared after a temperature change.
column7: There are multiple crazing lines and a growing crack in the windshield.
column8: Evaluate and replace the windshield or window per Cessna Service Manual and AC 43.13-1B. Small cracks can sometimes be stop-drilled (a small hole drilled at the tip of the crack to prevent propagation) — this is a temporary fix per approved data. If the crack is in the pilot's primary field of vision, or if it exceeds allowable length/location limits, the panel must be replaced. Cessna 172 windshields are acrylic (Plexiglas). Replacement involves removing the old panel, cleaning the mounting frame, and installing the new panel with approved sealant and mounting hardware. NOTE: Windshield crazing (fine surface cracks from UV degradation) reduces visibility, especially at night. Treat windshields with an approved plastic polish to extend life and reduce UV damage. Never use ammonia-based cleaners on acrylic — they accelerate crazing.

column4: My seat slid backward during takeoff rotation — the seat track locks didn't hold.
column5: The pilot seat slipped back during a go-around — I couldn't reach the controls fully.
column6: My seat moves on the tracks and doesn't lock securely in position — it's scary.
column7: The seat tracks are worn and the seat doesn't lock in all positions reliably.
column8: CRITICAL SAFETY ISSUE: Inspect and repair/replace the seat tracks and locking mechanism. Seat track/lock failure has been the cause of numerous fatal accidents in Cessna aircraft — the seat slides aft during rotation or go-around, the pilot instinctively pulls the yoke back, the aircraft enters a stall, and there is insufficient altitude to recover. Per applicable ADs (and successor ADs), Cessna single-engine seat tracks and locking pins must be inspected per the applicable Cessna Service Bulletin at every annual/100-hour inspection. The inspection includes: (1) Visual inspection of the seat rails for wear, corrosion, and damage. (2) Verify ALL locking pin positions engage securely. (3) Apply a forward and aft load to the seat and verify it does not move from any pin position. (4) Replace worn seat rails or pin assemblies. NOTE: This is one of the most important recurring inspections on the Cessna 172. ALWAYS verify your seat is locked before every flight — push back against the seat to confirm it's secure.

column4: My 1969 172 doesn't have shoulder harnesses — are they required?
column5: I want to add shoulder harnesses to my older Skyhawk for safety.
column6: The shoulder harness inertia reels aren't working properly — they don't lock when they should.
column7: My shoulder belts are frayed and need replacement.
column8: Shoulder harnesses: The 1969-1974 172 models may or may not have factory shoulder harnesses. Per FAR 91.105, shoulder harnesses must be used if installed. For aircraft without shoulder harnesses, retrofit kits are available (quality FAA-PMA approved replacements). Installation requires an STC or approved data and must be performed by an A&P/IA. For existing harnesses: inspect the webbing for fraying, cuts, UV degradation, and hardware for corrosion at every annual. Inertia reels must lock when subjected to rapid deceleration — test by jerking the belt sharply. Replace reels that don't lock reliably. NOTE: Shoulder harnesses dramatically improve survivability in a crash. The 1975 172M (and later) included inertia-reel shoulder harnesses as standard equipment. If your aircraft doesn't have them, a retrofit is one of the most important safety upgrades you can make.

column4: There's oil weeping from the back of the engine around the magneto and accessory case area.
column5: Oil drips from the accessory case — the gasket between the case halves seems to be leaking.
column6: The back of the engine is constantly wet with oil from the accessory section.
column7: Oil leaks from the magneto mount pad and the accessory case joints.
column8: Replace the accessory case gasket(s) and magneto mount pad gaskets. Disassemble the affected area, clean all mating surfaces, install new gaskets (Lycoming or approved equivalent), and reassemble. Torque all fasteners per Lycoming overhaul manual specifications. Also check the accessory case mounting studs for looseness. NOTE: Oil leaks from the accessory case often appear worse than they are due to propwash spreading the oil. However, any oil leak must be addressed — oil on the exhaust system is a fire hazard, and progressive oil loss can lead to dangerously low oil levels.

column4: My fuel gauges read full even when the tanks are half empty — I can't trust them.
column5: The fuel quantity indicators are wildly inaccurate — they show more fuel than I actually have.
column6: My fuel gauges are unreliable — one reads empty when there's still 10 gallons in the tank.
column7: I don't trust my fuel gauges at all — they give random readings.
column8: Calibrate or replace the fuel quantity indicating system. The 172 uses capacitance-type fuel quantity senders in the wing tanks connected to cockpit gauges. Common failures: (1) Corroded sender plates — remove, clean, and verify sender resistance per the Cessna 172 Service Manual wiring diagram. (2) Faulty gauge — check by substituting a known resistance at the gauge input. (3) Ground connection — corroded ground causes erratic readings. Per FAR 91.205, fuel quantity indicators are required equipment. However, per FAR 23.1337, the gauges are only required to be accurate at EMPTY. The POH states: fuel quantity indicators should be used only as an approximate indication. CRITICAL NOTE: NEVER rely solely on fuel gauges for fuel management. Use time-based fuel calculations (burn rate × time) as the PRIMARY method. The gauges are a secondary reference only. Running out of fuel is one of the most common causes of GA accidents — and it is 100% preventable.

column4: The ELT battery is expired and the unit failed its annual test.
column5: My Emergency Locator Transmitter won't activate during the annual check — the battery is dead.
column6: The ELT is overdue for battery replacement and didn't pass its functional test.
column7: My ELT needs a new battery and it failed the annual inspection test.
column8: Replace the ELT battery and test the unit. Per FAR 91.207, the ELT must be inspected within 12 months of previous inspection. The battery must be replaced when the regulatory limit has been used or when the transmitter has been activated in an emergency. Replace with the correct battery per the ELT manufacturer. After battery replacement, perform a functional test — per FAR 91.207(d), ELT tests are only permitted during the the regulatory timeframe and shall not exceed the regulatory limit. Verify the unit is properly registered with NOAA (406 MHz ELTs). NOTE: If your 172 still has an older 121.5 MHz ELT, consider upgrading to a 406 MHz ELT — 121.5 MHz units are no longer monitored by satellite. A 406 MHz ELT with GPS provides precise position data to SAR (Search and Rescue) within minutes.

column4: My tachometer reads inconsistently — it shows different RPMs than what I know the engine is producing.
column5: The tach seems to read low — my cruise RPMs are lower than expected for the power setting.
column6: My mechanic says the tachometer is inaccurate — it needs to be checked against a known-good reference.
column7: The tach needle bounces and gives unreliable readings.
column8: Check and calibrate or replace the tachometer. The 172I-M uses a mechanical tach driven by a flexible cable from the engine accessory case. Common issues: (1) The tach cable is worn, kinked, or dry — lubricate or replace the cable. (2) The tach mechanism is worn — replace or overhaul the instrument. (3) Verify accuracy with a hand-held digital tachometer (photo-tach or laser tach) at several RPM settings. Maximum allowable error: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. NOTE: An inaccurate tachometer can cause over-revving (engine damage) or under-revving (power loss). It also affects mixture management since many pilots lean by RPM drop. Verify tach accuracy at each annual.

column4: My wheel fairings (pants) are cracked and loose — they rattle and pieces are coming off.
column5: The wheel fairings got damaged from a rough runway and one is about to fall off.
column6: My gear leg fairings are cracked and the mounting brackets are broken.
column7: The wheel pants are deteriorated — cracks, holes, and loose attachment hardware.
column8: Remove, inspect, and repair or replace the wheel fairings. Cracked fairings can be repaired with approved fiberglass repair methods (per AC 43.13-1B). Verify all mounting brackets, screws, and safety wire are intact and properly secured. Loose or detached fairings in flight can jam the rudder cables or become FOD on the runway. NOTE: Many operators remove the wheel fairings for training operations or rough-field use to prevent damage and reduce maintenance. This is acceptable — the fairings are not required for airworthiness. However, removing them reduces cruise speed by approximately 3-5 knots due to increased drag.

column4: The starter engages but won't turn the engine — I hear a grinding noise and the starter just spins.
column5: My starter makes a terrible grinding sound but the engine doesn't turn over.
column6: The starter motor runs but doesn't engage the engine — something between the starter and the engine has failed.
column7: There's a grinding noise from the starter area and the engine won't crank.
column8: Inspect the starter adapter and flywheel ring gear. The Lycoming O-320 uses a starter adapter assembly that engages the ring gear. Common failures: (1) Worn or damaged starter adapter clutch — the adapter contains an overrunning clutch that can wear, preventing proper engagement. Replace the starter adapter (Lycoming the correct FAA-PMA approved or OEM replacement part or current replacement). (2) Damaged ring gear teeth — if teeth are chipped or worn, the starter can't engage. Replace the ring gear (requires engine removal). (3) Worn starter drive gear (Bendix drive). Replace the starter or starter drive. NOTE: A grinding starter that doesn't turn the engine is NOT a minor issue — continued attempts can further damage the ring gear and adapter. Diagnose before attempting more starts.

column4: The trim wheel is stiff and the trim doesn't respond smoothly — it's hard to get the airplane properly trimmed.
column5: My elevator trim doesn't work properly — it's either too nose-up or too nose-down with nothing in between.
column6: The trim cable feels gritty and the trim tab moves in jerks instead of smoothly.
column7: The elevator trim is very hard to adjust in flight — the trim wheel barely moves.
column8: Inspect and service/replace the elevator trim system. The 172 uses a cable-actuated trim tab on the elevator. Common issues: (1) Trim cable wear/corrosion — replace the trim cable if frayed or corroded. (2) Trim cable pulleys — inspect for wear, flat spots, and bearing failure. Replace as needed. (3) Trim tab actuator — the jackscrew mechanism on the tab can become dry or corroded. Lubricate with approved lubricant. (4) Trim wheel mechanism — the drum and cable can become stiff from dirt/corrosion inside the cabin mechanism. Clean and lubricate. After any trim system work, verify full travel and correct sense (wheel forward = nose down, wheel aft = nose up). Check the trim tab for security and free play within limits. NOTE: An inoperative or jammed trim system affects controllability — per the POH, the airplane can be flown without trim, but pilot workload increases significantly, especially on long flights.

column3: Carburetor Heat Control Cable Stiff / Broken
column4: The carb heat knob is very stiff to pull and doesn't seem to give full heat — or it broke off in my hand.
column5: My carburetor heat control cable is binding and I can't get full carb heat.
column6: The carb heat cable broke and I have no carburetor heat available.
column7: The carb heat knob is almost impossible to pull — the cable is seized or kinked.
column8: Replace or service the carburetor heat control cable. The carb heat cable operates a valve on the carburetor air box that diverts intake air through a shroud around the exhaust to pre-heat it. If the cable is stiff: lubricate with cable lubricant and check for kinks in the routing. If the cable is broken or permanently stuck: replace the cable (the correct replacement per the Cessna IPC for your specific model). After replacement, verify full travel — the valve must open completely for full carb heat and close completely for cold air. CRITICAL: Carburetor heat is a REQUIRED safety system. An inoperative carb heat cable is a grounding item — the aircraft cannot be dispatched until it's repaired. Carburetor ice can cause complete engine failure, and carb heat is the only means of preventing and removing it on the O-320-E2D installation.

column4: The engine runs rough and the mechanic found that the magneto timing has drifted from the specification.
column5: My magnetos were retimed during the annual — they had drifted significantly from the correct setting.
column6: The engine performance has degraded and the mag timing was found to be off during the 100-hour inspection.
column7: The magneto timing was incorrect — the mechanic reset it and the engine runs much better now.
column8: Retime the magnetos. The O-320-E2D specification is within the limits specified in the applicable service publication (both magnetos). Use a timing light/buzzer (a quality timing device) to verify and set the timing. Set the crankshaft to the correct position using the timing marks on the flywheel/ring gear. Adjust the magneto body position (internal point gap affects timing — points must be gapped per the mag manufacturer before timing). Verify both mags fire at within the limits specified in the applicable service publication. NOTE: Magneto timing can drift due to: (1) Point wear changing the point gap over time. (2) Magneto mounting hardware loosening. (3) Impulse coupling wear. Timing should be verified at every annual/100-hour inspection and after any magneto service.

column4: My altimeter doesn't match the field elevation when I set the current altimeter setting — it's off by over 75 feet.
column5: The altimeter reads incorrectly — there's a persistent error that doesn't go away with the correct Kollsman setting.
column6: My altimeter is inaccurate — the IFR inspection found it out of tolerance.
column7: The altimeter error exceeds the allowable limits for IFR flight.
column8: Have the altimeter inspected and calibrated by a certified instrument repair station. Per FAR 91.411, the altimeter must be inspected every 24 calendar months for IFR operations. The inspection verifies accuracy at multiple pressure altitudes and checks for scale error, hysteresis, and friction error. Allowable tolerance: within the limits specified in the applicable service publication at sea level. If the altimeter is out of tolerance, it must be overhauled/calibrated or replaced. NOTE: Even for VFR flight, an inaccurate altimeter is a safety concern — incorrect altitude indications can lead to CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain) or airspace violations. Per FAR 91.205, an adjustable altimeter is required for all flight.

column4: The mechanic found corrosion at the wing strut attach fittings during the annual inspection.
column5: There's corrosion where the wing struts connect to the fuselage and wing — it looks like water has been getting in.
column6: The wing strut attachments show corrosion and the mechanic is concerned about structural integrity.
column7: Corrosion was found at the wing strut fittings — the attach bolts and surrounding structure are affected.
column8: Inspect and remediate per Cessna Service Manual and applicable inspection criteria. The wing strut attach fittings are critical structural components — the struts carry a significant portion of the wing loads.
